rationale: The citing paper gives Roehm and Tybout (2006) a clear functional role as prior evidence that spillovers can occur across competing brands, and it discusses specific limits of that evidence (lab setting and scandal-only negative information). It also uses the cited paper in its conceptual setup for the accessibility-diagnosticity logic behind cross-brand spillovers. The 2009 study then positions its own contribution as testing and modeling these spillovers in real market choice data and broader informational contexts, which goes beyond a passing mention.
relation_description: The citing paper extends Roehm and Tybout (2006) from an experimental brand-scandal context to a structural Bayesian learning analysis of real physician choice data. It broadens the line of work by modeling prior and dynamic spillovers and testing similarity as a moderator outside scandal-only stimuli.
